title: City Life
text: The city is crowded with workers, carts and smoke. Families live close to factories because work is nearby, but clean water and quiet streets are hard to find.
historyNote: Urbanisation grew as people moved to industrial cities for work. Cities offered wages but often had overcrowding and poor sanitation.
evidence: Industrial cities offered work but also created serious housing and health problems.

choices.label: Look for work near the factories.
Investigate the crowded housing.
Attend a meeting about city conditions.

title: Steam Power
text: A steam engine pumps and turns beside a factory wall. It can power machines even when there is no river nearby. Coal keeps it moving.
historyNote: Steam power helped factories, mines, ships and railways grow. It made industry less dependent on water power.
evidence: Steam power allowed factories and transport to expand beyond rivers.

choices.label: Study how the engine works.
Take paid work moving coal.
Ask if steam power makes work safer.

title: Canals and Railways
text: At the wharf, barges carry coal and cotton along canals. Nearby, workers prepare iron rails for a new railway. Goods are moving faster than ever.
historyNote: Canals and railways helped move raw materials and finished goods. Better transport connected mines, factories, ports and markets.
evidence: Improved transport helped industrial Britain move goods faster and more cheaply.

choices.label: Map how cotton travels to the mill.
Work at the canal wharf for wages.
Ask workers how railways may change their jobs.

title: Smoke and Dirty Water
text: Coal smoke darkens the sky. Waste water runs near the street. Some people say pollution is the price of progress, but others cough through the working day.
historyNote: Industrial growth caused air and water pollution. Public health problems became worse in crowded cities with poor drains.
evidence: Industrial growth brought pollution that harmed health and city life.

choices.label: Collect evidence about smoke and sickness.
Ignore the smoke and keep earning wages.
Call for cleaner water and drains.

title: Protest in the Street
text: Workers gather after hearing that machines may reduce jobs. Some want a peaceful petition. Others are angry enough to damage property.
historyNote: Industrial change led to protests about wages, working conditions and machines. Protest could be peaceful or violent.
evidence: Protest showed that not everyone benefited equally from industrial change.

choices.label: Support a peaceful petition.
Join the angry crowd.
Speak to workers about safer ways to organise.

title: The Reform Meeting
text: Workers, parents, doctors and reformers meet in a crowded hall. They discuss factory safety, child labour, cleaner streets and the need for laws.
historyNote: Reform meetings helped spread ideas and organise pressure for change. Reformers often used evidence, speeches and petitions.
evidence: Reform movements used meetings and evidence to push for better laws and conditions.

choices.label: Speak about factory safety.
Speak about dirty water and disease.
Collect signatures for a petition.

title: City Sanitation
text: A doctor explains that dirty water can spread disease. Families want drains, clean pumps and rubbish removed from the streets.
historyNote: Public health reform focused on sanitation, clean water and disease prevention in crowded industrial cities.
evidence: City sanitation became a public issue because disease spread through crowded and dirty streets.

choices.label: Send sanitation evidence to Parliament.
Organise neighbours to clean the street.
Return to the reform meeting with your findings.

title: Cotton Across Oceans
text: A map shows cotton travelling from distant lands to British mills. The cloth in shops depends on farms, ships, ports and empire.
historyNote: Britain's cotton industry was connected to empire and global trade. Cotton supplies linked factories to people and places far beyond Britain.
evidence: British industrial growth depended on global cotton supplies and overseas trade.

choices.label: Trace the cotton route carefully.
Ask who profits from cotton trade.
Use this evidence in your final judgement.

title: Parliament Debates Reform
text: Parliament hears arguments about factories, protest, sanitation and child labour. Some leaders want stronger laws. Others fear that rules will hurt business.
historyNote: Parliament gradually passed reforms such as Factory Acts and public health measures, but change was often slow and contested.
evidence: Government reform grew from evidence, public pressure and debate about the costs of industrialisation.

choices.label: Support Factory Acts to protect workers.
Support public health laws for cleaner cities.
Argue that industry brought both benefits and harms.

title: Final Reflection
text: Use your evidence to answer: Was the Industrial Revolution mainly positive, mainly negative, or mixed?
historyNote: A strong historical judgement uses evidence from different viewpoints. It explains benefits, harms and who experienced them.
evidence: A balanced reflection should use evidence about work, cities, transport, pollution, protest and reform.
